title: Core Spring & Spring Boot
overview: Spring Boot is a framework for building Java backend applications quickly.
It is built directly on top of the Spring Framework.
It removes the manual setup work that plain Spring required.
why: Every backend needs dependency wiring, web request handling, and configuration.
The Spring Framework provides all of this.
But wiring it all together by hand is slow and repetitive.
problem: **Plain Spring** required heavy XML configuration.
Every bean had to be declared by hand in `applicationContext.xml`.
You had to manually pick compatible versions of every library.
A version mismatch between two libraries could break the whole app.
You built a WAR file.
You deployed that WAR file to an externally-installed Tomcat server.
All of this happened before you wrote a single line of business logic.

intro: Spring Boot solves the problem above in a few specific ways.

topics.heading: What Spring Boot Actually Adds
points: **Auto-configuration** — Spring Boot looks at what's on the classpath and configures sensible defaults automatically.
**Starter dependencies** — one dependency, `spring-boot-starter-web`, pulls in a whole set of version-matched libraries.
**Embedded server** — Tomcat ships inside the JAR file itself.
Running the app becomes one command: `java -jar app.jar`.
**Actuator** — production-ready monitoring endpoints, with no extra code.

topics.heading: The Entry Point
points: **`@SpringBootApplication`** is actually three annotations combined into one.
`@Configuration` — this class is allowed to define beans.
`@EnableAutoConfiguration` — turn on auto-configuration.
`@ComponentScan` — scan this package, and everything below it, for `@Component` classes.
**`SpringApplication.run(App.class, args)`** starts the whole application.
It scans for components, applies auto-configuration, starts the embedded server, and builds the `ApplicationContext`.

topics.heading: Behind the Scenes: What SpringApplication.run() Actually Does
points: **`SpringApplication`** is the class orchestrating the whole startup — it decides the application type, prepares the environment, and creates the `ApplicationContext`.
**`ApplicationContextInitializer`** — hook implementations that run against the context before it's refreshed, useful for programmatic setup.
**`ConfigurationClassPostProcessor`** — a `BeanFactoryPostProcessor` that scans `@Configuration` classes and registers their `@Bean` methods as bean definitions.
**`AutoConfigurationImportSelector`** — decides which auto-configuration classes actually get imported, filtering candidates through `@Conditional` checks.
Together, these four pieces turn one `SpringApplication.run()` call into a fully wired `ApplicationContext`.

topics.heading: Auto-Configuration Internals
points: **`@Conditional`** is the base mechanism — a configuration class or bean only registers if its condition is true.
**`@ConditionalOnClass`** — only apply this config if a given class is on the classpath.
**`@ConditionalOnMissingBean`** — only apply this config if the developer hasn't already defined their own bean of that type.
**`@ConditionalOnProperty`** — only apply this config if a specific property is set to a specific value.
Every candidate auto-configuration class is listed in `META-INF/spring/...AutoConfiguration.imports` (the older `spring.factories` served the same purpose).
`AutoConfigurationImportSelector` reads that full candidate list, then filters it down using each class's `@Conditional` checks.

topics.heading: Execution Timeline: Startup Sequence
topics.mermaid:
sequenceDiagram
    participant Main as "main()"
    participant SA as "SpringApplication"
    participant Env as "Environment"
    participant CCPP as "ConfigurationClassPostProcessor"
    participant ACIS as "AutoConfigurationImportSelector"
    participant Ctx as "ApplicationContext"
    participant Server as "Embedded Server"
    Main->>SA: run(PaymentServiceApplication.class, args)
    SA->>Env: prepare Environment, publish ApplicationEnvironmentPreparedEvent
    SA->>Ctx: create ApplicationContext, publish ApplicationContextInitializedEvent
    Ctx->>CCPP: process Configuration classes, register Bean definitions
    Ctx->>ACIS: resolve and filter auto-configuration candidates via Conditional checks
    SA->>Ctx: publish ApplicationPreparedEvent
    Ctx->>Ctx: refresh - instantiate singletons, publish ContextRefreshedEvent
    Ctx->>Server: start embedded Tomcat
    SA->>SA: publish ApplicationStartedEvent, then ApplicationReadyEvent
topics.mermaidCaption: A more granular view of the same startup shown in the diagram above — auto-configuration resolution and application events now placed in sequence.
points: This is the same overall flow as the diagram at the top of this page, just with the internal classes and events placed on a timeline.
Auto-configuration resolution happens **before** the context refresh finishes instantiating singleton beans.
The embedded server starts as part of the context refresh, not before it.
`ApplicationStartedEvent` and `ApplicationReadyEvent` both fire only after the server is already listening.

topics.heading: Application Events
points: Spring Boot publishes its own lifecycle events, in order: `ApplicationStartingEvent` → `ApplicationEnvironmentPreparedEvent` → `ApplicationContextInitializedEvent` → `ApplicationPreparedEvent` → `ContextRefreshedEvent` → `ApplicationStartedEvent` → `ApplicationReadyEvent`.
Any bean can observe these with `@EventListener`, or by implementing `ApplicationListener<T>`.
**`ApplicationReadyEvent`** is the common hook for "run this once the app is fully ready to serve traffic" logic — the server is already up at this point.
Events are published through the same `ApplicationEventPublisher` used for any other custom application event.

topics.heading: CommandLineRunner vs ApplicationRunner
points: Both run code right after the `ApplicationContext` is built and the app is basically ready, but before it starts serving requests.
**`CommandLineRunner.run(String... args)`** — receives the raw, unparsed `String` arguments exactly as passed on the command line.
**`ApplicationRunner.run(ApplicationArguments args)`** — receives a parsed `ApplicationArguments` object, with named options and non-option arguments already split out.
If multiple runners exist, `@Order` (or implementing `Ordered`) controls which one runs first.

topics.heading: Production Notes
points: Gate readiness/traffic checks on `ApplicationReadyEvent` rather than assuming the app is instantly ready the moment `main()` returns.
Use `CommandLineRunner`/`ApplicationRunner` for one-off startup tasks like cache warming — never for request-handling logic.
Keep auto-configuration overrides explicit and documented — silent overrides via `@ConditionalOnMissingBean` are a common source of "why is this bean different in prod" confusion.
Prefer the newer `META-INF/spring/...AutoConfiguration.imports` file over `spring.factories` in any auto-configuration module you own.

architectureFlow.mermaid:
graph TD
    A[main] --> B[SpringApplication.run]
    B --> C[Component Scan]
    B --> D[Auto-Configuration]
    B --> E[Embedded Server Starts]
    C --> F[ApplicationContext - IoC Container]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[App Running on port 8080]
architectureFlow.caption: SpringApplication.run() triggers three things at once, all feeding into the ApplicationContext.

simpleExample.scenario: The smallest possible Spring Boot application.
simpleExample.execution: `SpringApplication.run()` scans the `com.example.paymentservice` package, finds every `@Component`, wires them together, and starts an embedded Tomcat.
simpleExample.output: A running HTTP server, ready to accept requests, with no XML file and no external Tomcat install.
simpleExample.explanation: This one file, plus a build tool config, is a complete deployable Spring Boot application.

realWorldExample.scenario: Adding a new REST endpoint to an existing payment service with zero extra configuration.
realWorldExample.input: A POST request to `/payments` with a JSON body describing the payment.
realWorldExample.execution: Component scan finds `PaymentController` automatically. Auto-configuration already wired up the JSON parser and the embedded server's routing.
realWorldExample.output: A JSON response with the created `Payment`, with zero manual servlet or router configuration written.
realWorldExample.explanation: No `web.xml`, no manual servlet registration, no JSON library configuration — Spring Boot's auto-configuration already did that work before this class was even written.

comparisonTables.title: Spring Framework vs Spring Boot
rows: Manual XML or Java config for every bean
Pick and match every library version yourself
One starter dependency pulls in a matched set
Hours of configuration before writing logic
Minutes — a working app in one file

comparisonTables.title: CommandLineRunner vs ApplicationRunner
rows: Parsed — option and non-option arguments split out
Simple scripts that don't need named options

bestPractices: Let auto-configuration do its job — override a default only when you have a specific reason.
Keep the `@SpringBootApplication` class at the root package so component scanning covers everything below it.
Use starter dependencies (`spring-boot-starter-*`) instead of adding individual libraries by hand.
commonMistakes: Placing `@SpringBootApplication` in a sub-package — component scanning won't find classes outside its own package tree.
Manually adding a library version that conflicts with the one a starter already manages.
Assuming Spring Boot replaces the Spring Framework — it's built on top of it, not instead of it.
interviewTips: If asked "what is Spring Boot", lead with: it's Spring Framework plus auto-configuration, an embedded server, and starter dependencies — not a separate framework.
Be ready to explain what each of the three annotations inside `@SpringBootApplication` does individually.

faq.q: Does Spring Boot replace the Spring Framework?
faq.a: No. Spring Boot is built on top of the Spring Framework. You still use Spring's DI/IoC container, AOP, and MVC — Spring Boot just removes the manual setup around them.

faq.q: Can a Spring Boot app still use XML configuration?
faq.a: Yes, XML config is still supported for backward compatibility, but it's rarely used in new code — annotation-based and Java-based configuration are the default now.

faq.q: What happens if I don't include spring-boot-starter-web?
faq.a: Auto-configuration won't set up Spring MVC or an embedded servlet container, since there's no web starter on the classpath signaling that this is a web application.

summary: Spring Boot is Spring Framework plus auto-configuration, starter dependencies, an embedded server, and Actuator.
It removes the XML configuration, manual dependency matching, and external server setup that plain Spring required.
The entry point is one `@SpringBootApplication`-annotated class with a `main` method calling `SpringApplication.run()`.
quickRevision: Spring Boot is built on top of Spring Framework, not a replacement for it.
Problem it solves: heavy XML config, manual library version matching, external server deployment.
`@SpringBootApplication` = `@Configuration` + `@EnableAutoConfiguration` + `@ComponentScan`.
`SpringApplication.run()` triggers component scan, auto-configuration, and embedded server startup.
Auto-configuration reads the classpath and picks sensible defaults automatically.
Starter dependencies bundle a matched set of compatible libraries under one dependency.
The embedded server means `java -jar app.jar` is enough to run the app.
Actuator adds production monitoring endpoints with no extra code.

beginner: What is Spring Boot, in one sentence?
What does `@SpringBootApplication` actually consist of?
How do you run a Spring Boot application?
intermediate: What is auto-configuration, and how does it decide what to configure?
What's the practical difference between deploying a Spring app and a Spring Boot app?
What does a starter dependency actually provide?
advanced: How would you override a specific auto-configured bean without disabling auto-configuration entirely?
What happens internally when `SpringApplication.run()` is called, in order?
How does component scanning behave if `@SpringBootApplication` is placed in a non-root package?

relatedTopics.label: DI & IoC — how ApplicationContext wires dependencies
Bean Lifecycle & Scopes — what happens after the context is built
JioOnePay Overview — a real Spring Boot application

title: DI & IoC
overview: IoC and DI are two related but different ideas.
IoC is a principle. DI is the technique Spring uses to apply it.
This page covers both, plus exactly how Spring Boot wires classes together at runtime.
why: Every class in a backend needs other classes to do its job.
Something has to decide how those classes get created and connected.
IoC and DI are Spring's answer to that decision.
problem: **Without IoC**, a class creates its own dependencies directly.
`class OrderService { private PaymentGateway gateway = new RazorpayGateway(); }`.
`OrderService` decides which implementation it gets, and creates it itself.
Swapping `RazorpayGateway` for a different gateway means editing this class.
Testing `OrderService` alone always drags in a real `RazorpayGateway` too.
This tight coupling makes the code harder to change and harder to test.

intro: IoC is the goal. DI is how Spring actually reaches it. Here's each piece.

topics.heading: How It Looks After IoC
points: `class OrderService { private final PaymentGateway gateway; OrderService(PaymentGateway gateway) { this.gateway = gateway; } }`.
`OrderService` no longer creates anything.
It no longer knows which implementation it will get.
Something outside it — the IoC container — decides, and hands the dependency in.

topics.heading: Types of IoC
points: **Dependency Injection** — the container pushes dependencies into your class. This is what Spring uses.
**Dependency Lookup** — the older Service Locator style. Your class actively asks a registry for what it needs, e.g. `context.getBean(...)`.
DI is preferred because the class stays completely unaware of the container.
With Dependency Lookup, the class still has to know about, and call, the registry.

topics.heading: IoC Container: BeanFactory vs ApplicationContext
topics.imageAlt: Diagram showing BeanFactory as the root IoC container interface with lazy initialization, and ApplicationContext extending it with eager singleton initialization, events, internationalization, and AOP, which is what Spring Boot uses
points: The IoC container is the concrete engine that performs the inversion.
It creates objects and wires their dependencies, based on bean definitions it discovers.
Spring ships two container implementations.
**`BeanFactory`** — the root, most basic interface. Lazy — a bean is only created the first time `getBean()` actually requests it.
**`ApplicationContext`** — extends `BeanFactory`. Eagerly initializes every `singleton` at startup, so a broken bean fails fast, at boot.
It also adds event publishing, internationalization, and AOP integration.
**What we use today** — always `ApplicationContext`. Raw `BeanFactory` is essentially legacy.
**Key methods** — `getBean(Class<T> type)`, `containsBean(String name)`, `isSingleton(String name)`, `getBeanNamesForType(Class<?> type)`.
**Added by `ApplicationContext`** — `publishEvent(ApplicationEvent event)`, `getMessage(String code, Object[] args, Locale locale)`.

topics.heading: Types of Dependency Injection
topics.imageAlt: Diagram showing the IoC container turning bean definitions into wired objects, with a concrete example of UserController depending on UserService depending on UserRepository, all injected automatically
points: **Constructor injection (preferred)** — the dependency arrives as a constructor parameter.
Fields can be `final`. A missing dependency fails fast, at startup.
It's trivial to unit test — `new OrderService(mockGateway)`, no Spring needed at all.
**Setter injection** — the dependency arrives through a setter method.
Use this only for genuinely optional dependencies.
**Field injection (avoid)** — `@Autowired` goes directly on the field.
The field can't be `final`.
It hides the class's real dependencies from its constructor signature.

topics.heading: How Spring Boot Resolves & Wires Dependencies
points: For `@Autowired`, Spring first matches **by type**.
If more than one bean of that type exists, it then matches **by field/parameter name**.
Still ambiguous after that? It throws `NoUniqueBeanDefinitionException`.
**Implicit constructor injection** — a class with exactly one constructor doesn't need `@Autowired` on it at all.
**`@Qualifier("emailService")`** — picks a specific bean by name when several implement the same type.
**`@Primary`** — marks one bean as the default, used whenever no `@Qualifier` says otherwise.
**`@Lazy`** — defers creating a bean until it's first actually used.

topics.heading: Ways to Configure Beans for DI
points: **Component scanning** — annotate a class `@Component` (or `@Service`/`@Repository`/`@Controller`).
`@ComponentScan`, implied by `@SpringBootApplication`, auto-discovers it.
**Java Config** — `@Configuration class AppConfig { @Bean RestTemplate restTemplate() { ... } }`.
Needed for third-party classes you can't annotate yourself.
**XML config (legacy)** — `<bean id="orderService" class="..."/>`. Rarely used in new code today.

topics.heading: Behind the Scenes: Which Classes Actually Perform DI
points: **`ConfigurationClassPostProcessor`** — processes every `@Configuration` class first, registering each `@Bean` method as a bean definition.
**`DefaultListableBeanFactory`** — the concrete `BeanFactory` underneath `ApplicationContext`. It holds all bean definitions and actually instantiates beans.
**`AutowiredAnnotationBeanPostProcessor`** — a `BeanPostProcessor` that scans each bean as it's created and injects whatever its `@Autowired` fields or constructor need.
**Order matters** — bean definitions are registered first, then `AutowiredAnnotationBeanPostProcessor` acts during each individual bean's creation, not before.
This is why a bad `@Bean` method fails fast at startup, while a missing `@Autowired` candidate surfaces later, as `NoUniqueBeanDefinitionException`.

topics.heading: Production Notes
points: **Constructor injection scales well in teams** — a class's required dependencies are all visible in one place, its constructor signature.
Treat `@Lazy` as a warning sign, not a fix — a circular dependency between `OrderService` and `PaymentGateway` usually means refactor, not `@Lazy`.
Keep shared interfaces like `PaymentGateway` in a common module when multiple services depend on the same abstraction.
Favor one `@Primary` implementation per environment over scattering `@Qualifier` everywhere — it keeps most call sites simple.
Constructor injection keeps unit tests plain — `new OrderService(fakeGateway)` needs no Spring context at all.

architectureFlow.caption: The exact order Spring follows when it resolves an @Autowired dependency.

simpleExample.scenario: Injecting a payment gateway when only one implementation exists.
simpleExample.execution: Spring sees `OrderService`'s constructor needs a `PaymentGateway`, finds exactly one matching bean, and injects it. No `@Qualifier` needed.
simpleExample.output: A fully constructed `OrderService`, holding a working `RazorpayGateway` instance.
simpleExample.explanation: With only one candidate bean, type-based matching alone resolves the dependency.

realWorldExample.scenario: Injecting one specific gateway when two implementations exist — Razorpay and PhonePe.
realWorldExample.execution: Spring matches by type first, finds two candidates, then uses `@Qualifier("razorpayGateway")` to pick the exact one by name.
realWorldExample.output: `OrderService` is wired with `RazorpayGateway`, even though `PhonePeGateway` also exists in the container.
realWorldExample.explanation: Without `@Qualifier` here, Spring would throw `NoUniqueBeanDefinitionException` at startup, since two beans satisfy the same type.

comparisonTables.title: IoC vs DI
rows: A technique that implements the principle
Broader — control of the whole object lifecycle
The container performs DI to achieve IoC

comparisonTables.title: Constructor vs Setter vs Field Injection

bestPractices: Default to constructor injection for every required dependency.
Use `@Qualifier` only when a type genuinely has multiple valid implementations.
Reserve `@Primary` for the one implementation that should be the sensible default across the whole app.
commonMistakes: Using field injection because it's shorter to type — it hides real dependencies and blocks easy unit testing.
Forgetting `@Qualifier` when two beans implement the same interface, causing `NoUniqueBeanDefinitionException` at startup.
Confusing Dependency Lookup (you call `getBean()`) with Dependency Injection (the container calls you) — they're opposite directions of control.
interviewTips: If asked to define IoC and DI separately: IoC is the principle, DI is the technique Spring uses to implement it.
If asked why constructor injection is preferred, mention immutability, fail-fast behavior, and testability together.
Know the exact resolution order: type, then qualifier or primary, then name.

faq.q: Is Dependency Injection the only way to implement IoC?
faq.a: No. DI is the most common technique and the one Spring uses, but Dependency Lookup (Service Locator) is another way to implement the same IoC principle — the class actively looks up its dependency instead of receiving it.

faq.q: Why does Spring prefer constructor injection over field injection?
faq.a: Constructor injection lets fields be `final`, makes a missing dependency fail immediately at startup instead of with a NullPointerException later, and makes the class trivial to unit test without starting Spring at all.

faq.q: What happens if two beans of the same type exist with no @Primary or @Qualifier?
faq.a: Spring throws `NoUniqueBeanDefinitionException` at startup, because it can't decide which bean to inject.

summary: IoC is the principle: control over creating and wiring objects moves from your code to a container.
DI is the technique Spring uses to apply that principle: dependencies are pushed in via constructor, setter, or field.
Constructor injection is preferred for its immutability, fail-fast behavior, and testability.
Spring resolves an `@Autowired` dependency by type first, then by qualifier or primary status if there's more than one match.
quickRevision: IoC = principle. DI = technique. Spring uses DI to implement IoC.
Before IoC: a class creates its own dependencies with `new`. After IoC: something else hands them in.
Two IoC container types: `BeanFactory` (lazy) and `ApplicationContext` (eager, used by Spring Boot).
Three ways to inject: constructor (preferred), setter (optional deps), field (avoid).
Spring resolves `@Autowired` by type first, then by name if there's ambiguity.
`@Qualifier` picks a specific bean by name. `@Primary` sets a default.
`@Lazy` defers bean creation until first use.
Two beans of the same type with no `@Qualifier`/`@Primary` throws `NoUniqueBeanDefinitionException`.

beginner: What is the difference between IoC and DI?
Name the three ways to inject a dependency in Spring.
Why is constructor injection generally preferred?
intermediate: What's the difference between `BeanFactory` and `ApplicationContext`?
How does `@Qualifier` differ from `@Primary`?
What happens if Spring finds two beans of the same type with no way to disambiguate them?
advanced: How would you inject a `prototype`-scoped bean into a `singleton` bean and still get a fresh instance every time?
Walk through Spring's exact resolution order when multiple `@Autowired` candidates exist.
When would Dependency Lookup be a more appropriate choice than Dependency Injection?

relatedTopics.label: Core Spring & Spring Boot — how ApplicationContext is built at startup
Bean Lifecycle & Scopes — what happens after a dependency is injected
JioOnePay Overview — dependency injection in a real production system

title: Annotations Deep Dive
overview: Spring annotations replace manual configuration and repetitive boilerplate.
Each annotation tells Spring to do one specific job automatically.
This page covers the annotations used in almost every REST backend.
why: Without annotations, every bean, every route, and every validation rule needs manual wiring.
Annotations let you declare intent directly on the class or method.
Spring reads that intent and does the wiring for you.
problem: Before annotations, Spring configuration lived entirely in XML.
Declaring one bean meant writing an XML `<bean>` block by hand.
Mapping one URL to one method meant registering it in a separate config file.
The code and its configuration lived far apart from each other.
Annotations moved that configuration next to the code it actually affects.

intro: Five annotation groups cover almost everything a typical REST backend needs.

topics.heading: Stereotype Annotations
points: **`@Component`** — a generic Spring-managed bean. Every other stereotype builds on it.
It tells the container: create and manage one instance of this class.
**`@Service`** — same behavior as `@Component`, marking the business logic layer.
**`@Repository`** — marks the data access layer.
It also translates a raw JDBC `SQLException` into Spring's own `DataAccessException` automatically.
**`@Controller` / `@RestController`** — marks the web layer.
`@RestController` = `@Controller` + `@ResponseBody`.
Every method's return value is serialized straight to JSON, not resolved to a view template.

topics.heading: Request Mapping
points: **`@GetMapping("/orders/{id}")`** — read a resource. Shorthand for `@RequestMapping(method = GET)`.
**`@PostMapping("/orders")`** — create a resource; the request body carries the new data.
**`@PutMapping("/orders/{id}")`** — full replace of an existing resource.
**`@PatchMapping("/orders/{id}")`** — partial update; only the changed fields are sent.
**`@DeleteMapping("/orders/{id}")`** — delete a resource, typically returning `204 No Content`.

topics.heading: Request & Response Handling
points: **`@RequestBody`** — deserializes the incoming JSON body straight into a Java object.
**`@PathVariable`** — extracts a value embedded directly in the URL path.
**`@RequestParam`** — reads a query-string parameter, with an optional default value.
**`@RequestHeader`** — reads one specific HTTP header.
**`ResponseEntity<T>`** — full control over status code, headers, and body together.

topics.heading: Validation
points: **`@Valid` / `@Validated`** — trigger bean validation before the method body runs at all.
This rejects bad input before it ever reaches business logic.
**`@NotNull` / `@NotBlank` / `@NotEmpty`** — `@NotBlank` is for `String`s specifically.
It rejects `null`, empty string, and whitespace-only values.
**`@Size` / `@Min` / `@Max`** — bound a field's length or numeric range.
**`@Email` / `@Pattern`** — validate a format using a built-in or custom regex.
**Custom validator** — implement `ConstraintValidator` when a built-in rule isn't enough.

topics.heading: Global Exception Handling
points: **`@ControllerAdvice`** — one class centralizes exception handling across every controller.
**`@ExceptionHandler(NotFoundException.class)`** — inside that class, maps one exception type to one HTTP response.
This gives the whole API one consistent error-response shape.
**`ProblemDetail`** (Spring 6+) — a built-in RFC 7807 error format, instead of a hand-rolled error DTO.

topics.heading: Behind the Scenes: DispatcherServlet Request Lifecycle
points: **`DispatcherServlet`** — the single servlet that receives every incoming HTTP request; it's Spring MVC's Front Controller.
**`RequestMappingHandlerMapping`** — scans all `@Controller` beans and finds which method matches this request's path and HTTP method, based on `@GetMapping`/`@PostMapping`/etc.
**`RequestMappingHandlerAdapter`** — actually invokes the matched method, resolving `@RequestBody`/`@PathVariable`/`@RequestParam` arguments and processing the return value.
**`HandlerExceptionResolver`** — the internal mechanism `@ControllerAdvice` and `@ExceptionHandler` are built on top of; it catches exceptions the adapter throws and turns them into a response.
None of this is visible in application code — the annotations are just metadata these classes read at request time.

topics.heading: Execution Timeline: One Request, Start to Finish
points: This is the class-level machinery behind the annotations/validation flow shown in the architecture diagram above, traced request by request.
topics.mermaid:
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client
    participant DS as DispatcherServlet
    participant HM as RequestMappingHandlerMapping
    participant HA as RequestMappingHandlerAdapter
    participant OC as OrderController
    Client->>DS: HTTP request
    DS->>HM: Which handler matches this path/method?
    HM-->>DS: Matched OrderController.getOrder
    DS->>HA: Invoke this handler
    HA->>HA: Resolve PathVariable/RequestBody/RequestParam
    HA->>OC: Call getOrder(id)
    OC-->>HA: Return Order
    HA-->>DS: Serialize response body
    DS-->>Client: HTTP response
topics.mermaidCaption: The DispatcherServlet/HandlerMapping/HandlerAdapter machinery that runs underneath every annotated controller method.

topics.heading: Production Notes
points: **One `@ControllerAdvice` per API** (or per API version) — scattering exception handling across multiple advice classes makes it hard to guarantee every error looks the same.
**Use `ProblemDetail` consistently** — every error response across the whole platform should share one shape, not a different hand-rolled DTO per team.
**Decide API versioning early** — retrofitting a versioning strategy after clients are already integrated breaks them; it's much cheaper to decide upfront.
**Keep validation at the boundary** — DTO-level `@Valid` annotations belong on the request object, not duplicated as manual checks deeper in the service layer.

architectureFlow.mermaid:
graph TD
    A["HTTP Request"] --> B["Matched by GetMapping/PostMapping"]
    B --> C["RequestBody/PathVariable/RequestParam bind the data"]
    C --> D{"Valid passes?"}
    D -->|Yes| E["Controller method runs"]
    D -->|No| F["Validation exception thrown"]
    E --> G{"Exception thrown?"}
    G -->|No| H["Response returned"]
    G -->|Yes| F
    F --> I["Caught by ControllerAdvice"]
    I --> H
architectureFlow.caption: Every annotated request follows this same path — mapping, binding, validation, then either a normal response or a caught, consistent error response.

simpleExample.scenario: Reading a single order by its id.
simpleExample.execution: `@GetMapping("/{id}")` matches the path, `@PathVariable Long id` extracts `42`, and `orderService.findById(42)` runs.
simpleExample.output: A `200 OK` response with the `Order` serialized as JSON, thanks to `@RestController`.
simpleExample.explanation: No manual JSON conversion, no manual URL parsing — both are handled entirely by the annotations.

realWorldExample.scenario: Creating an order with validation, and a consistent error response if validation fails.
realWorldExample.execution: `@Valid` runs before the controller method body. `merchantId` fails `@NotNull`, so Spring throws `MethodArgumentNotValidException` instead of calling `create()`.
realWorldExample.output: A `400 Bad Request` with a `ProblemDetail` body, produced by the `@ControllerAdvice` handler — not a raw stack trace.
realWorldExample.explanation: The validation and the error-handling logic live in two small, focused places, instead of a giant `if` block inside the controller method.

comparisonTables.title: @Component vs @Service vs @Repository vs @Controller
rows: Resolves return values to view templates

bestPractices: Use `@RestController` for APIs and plain `@Controller` only when you're actually rendering a server-side view.
Validate at the boundary with `@Valid` — don't hand-write the same null/range checks again inside the service layer.
Centralize error handling in one `@ControllerAdvice` instead of repeating `try-catch` in every controller.
commonMistakes: Using `@RequestParam` when the value is really identifying a resource — that belongs in the URL path as `@PathVariable`.
Forgetting `@Valid` on a `@RequestBody` parameter — the validation annotations on the DTO are silently ignored without it.
Catching exceptions locally in every controller instead of using one shared `@ControllerAdvice`.
interviewTips: If asked to name the stereotype annotations, mention that they all behave like `@Component` and differ only in intent and, for `@Repository`, exception translation.
If asked about validation, be ready to explain that `@Valid` alone does nothing without a `@ControllerAdvice` (or try-catch) to handle the resulting exception.

faq.q: Is there a real functional difference between @Component and @Service?
faq.a: Functionally, no — Spring treats them identically as beans. The difference is purely semantic, for readability, except for `@Repository`, which adds real exception-translation behavior.

faq.q: What happens if @Valid fails and there's no @ControllerAdvice?
faq.a: Spring still returns a 400 response by default, using its own built-in error format — but it won't match your API's custom error shape unless you add a handler for it.

faq.q: Can one @ControllerAdvice handle exceptions for only some controllers?
faq.a: Yes — @ControllerAdvice accepts `basePackages` or `assignableTypes` to scope it to specific controllers instead of the whole application.

summary: Annotations move configuration next to the code they affect, replacing XML wiring.
Stereotype annotations mark a bean's role; request-mapping annotations map HTTP requests to methods.
`@Valid` plus `@ControllerAdvice` together turn bad input into a consistent, structured error response.
quickRevision: `@Component` is the base; `@Service`, `@Repository`, `@Controller` all build on it.
`@Repository` uniquely adds automatic persistence-exception translation.
`@RestController` = `@Controller` + `@ResponseBody`.
`@PathVariable` reads from the URL path; `@RequestParam` reads from the query string.
`@RequestBody` deserializes JSON straight into a Java object.
`@Valid` triggers validation; it does nothing on its own without a handler for the failure.
`@ControllerAdvice` + `@ExceptionHandler` centralize error handling across every controller.
`ProblemDetail` is Spring's built-in RFC 7807 error response format.

beginner: What is the difference between `@Controller` and `@RestController`?
What does `@PathVariable` do?
What annotation makes a class's dependencies get automatically wired by Spring?
intermediate: What extra behavior does `@Repository` add beyond `@Component`?
What happens if `@Valid` fails on a `@RequestBody` parameter?
Why would you use `@ControllerAdvice` instead of try-catch in each controller?
advanced: How would you scope a `@ControllerAdvice` to only a subset of controllers?
How would you write a custom validation annotation, end to end?
How does Spring decide which `@ExceptionHandler` method to call when an exception has multiple matching handlers in the class hierarchy?

relatedTopics.label: Core Spring & Spring Boot — how the embedded server starts
Spring Data JPA — validating and persisting the request body
JioOnePay Architecture Layers — a real request lifecycle across multiple services

title: Bean Lifecycle & Scopes
overview: Every Spring bean goes through the same predictable stages from creation to destruction.
A bean's scope decides how many instances of it exist at once.
Getting either one wrong is a common source of subtle production bugs.
why: Some setup logic can only run once all of a bean's dependencies are ready.
Some cleanup logic must run before the application shuts down.
Spring needs a defined lifecycle so setup and cleanup happen at the right moment, every time.
problem: Without a lifecycle, a constructor would be the only hook available.
A constructor runs **before** dependencies are injected.
That means a bean couldn't safely use its own dependencies inside its constructor.
There would also be no reliable moment to release resources like connections before shutdown.

intro: The lifecycle has five stages. Scope is a separate, related decision.

topics.heading: The Five Stages
points: **1. Constructor called** — the instance is created.
Dependencies are passed in here, if using constructor injection.
**2. Dependencies injected** — setter/field injection happens at this point.
**3. `@PostConstruct`** runs once, right after every dependency is set.
**4. Bean ready** — used for the rest of the application's life.
**5. `@PreDestroy`** runs once, when the container shuts down.

topics.heading: Bean Scopes
points: **`singleton`** (default) — exactly one instance per container, shared everywhere.
**`prototype`** — a new instance every time the bean is requested.
**`request`** — one instance per HTTP request.
**`session`** — one instance per HTTP session.
**`application`** — one instance per `ServletContext`.

topics.heading: The Prototype-in-Singleton Gotcha
points: A singleton bean is wired **once**, when the application starts.
If a `prototype` bean is injected directly into it, that injection also happens only once.
The result: one "prototype" instance, shared for the singleton's entire lifetime — the opposite of what `prototype` scope promises.
**Fix 1** — inject `ObjectProvider<T>` instead, and call `.getObject()` each time a fresh instance is needed.
**Fix 2** — use a scoped proxy: `@Scope(proxyMode = ScopedProxyMode.TARGET_CLASS)`.

topics.heading: Behind the Scenes: BeanPostProcessor & the Real Bean Creation Pipeline
points: "The Five Stages" is the simplified view — under the hood, every bean actually passes through the **`BeanPostProcessor`** interface.
**`postProcessBeforeInitialization`** runs right before `@PostConstruct`/`afterPropertiesSet` — most implementations just pass the bean through untouched.
**`postProcessAfterInitialization`** runs right after — this is exactly where Spring AOP proxies, like the ones behind `@Transactional`, get created and returned instead of the raw bean.
**`AutowiredAnnotationBeanPostProcessor`** is one concrete, already-registered `BeanPostProcessor` — it's the thing that actually handles `@Autowired` injection.

**`InitializingBean`**/**`DisposableBean`** are the older interface-based alternative to `@PostConstruct`/`@PreDestroy` — implement `afterPropertiesSet()`/`destroy()` directly instead of annotating a method.
Rarely used in new code since it couples a class to Spring-specific interfaces, but worth recognizing in legacy codebases.

topics.heading: Production Notes
points: Most teams never write a custom `BeanPostProcessor` themselves — it's a framework-level extension point, mostly exercised by Spring itself and by library authors.
`@PostConstruct` is the practical default over implementing `InitializingBean`, since annotating a method doesn't couple the class to a Spring-specific interface.
The prototype-in-singleton gotcha is a very common source of a subtle production bug — shared mutable state that looks scoped per-call but isn't.
If a proxy-based `@Transactional` bean isn't behaving as expected, `postProcessAfterInitialization` is the mental model to reach for: the object you're calling may not be the raw bean at all.

architectureFlow.mermaid:
graph TD
    A["Constructor called"] --> B["Dependencies injected"]
    B --> C["PostConstruct runs"]
    C --> D["Bean ready - in use"]
    D --> E["App shuts down"]
    E --> F["PreDestroy runs"]
architectureFlow.caption: The same five stages, every time, for every bean.

simpleExample.scenario: Validating a required config value right after Spring finishes injecting it.
simpleExample.execution: Spring constructs `PaymentGatewayClient`, injects the empty `apiKey`, then calls `validate()` as part of `@PostConstruct`.
simpleExample.output: The application fails to start immediately, with a clear `IllegalStateException` naming the missing property.
simpleExample.explanation: `@PostConstruct` guarantees this check runs only after Spring has finished injecting everything the bean needs — a constructor can't make that same guarantee as safely.

realWorldExample.scenario: A singleton CartService that needs a fresh CartCalculator on every call, not one shared instance.
realWorldExample.input: Two separate calls to `calculate()`, each with a different list of amounts.
realWorldExample.execution: Each call to `calculatorProvider.getObject()` asks the container for a brand-new `CartCalculator`, instead of reusing one shared instance.
realWorldExample.output: Each call gets its own `CartCalculator`, with its own independent running total starting at zero.
realWorldExample.explanation: If `CartCalculator` had been injected directly as a constructor parameter, `CartService` — a singleton — would have been wired with exactly one shared `CartCalculator`, mutated across every call. That's the prototype-in-singleton gotcha in practice.

comparisonTables.title: Bean Scopes
rows: Per-request state in a web app

bestPractices: Use `@PostConstruct` for setup logic that depends on injected fields being ready.
Use `@PreDestroy` to release resources like connections, threads, or file handles.
Reach for `ObjectProvider` or a scoped proxy the moment a prototype bean needs to go inside a singleton.
commonMistakes: Injecting a prototype bean as a plain constructor parameter into a singleton, expecting a fresh instance every call.
Doing setup work that depends on injected fields inside the constructor instead of `@PostConstruct`.
Forgetting that `@PreDestroy` never runs if the JVM is killed forcefully instead of shut down gracefully.
interviewTips: If asked to list the bean lifecycle stages, state them in strict order: construct, inject, `@PostConstruct`, ready, `@PreDestroy`.
The prototype-in-singleton gotcha is one of the most common Spring interview traps — be ready to explain it with a concrete example.

faq.q: Does @PreDestroy always run?
faq.a: No. It only runs on a graceful shutdown of the ApplicationContext. A forceful kill of the JVM skips it entirely.

faq.q: Why can't I just inject the prototype bean directly?
faq.a: A singleton bean is constructed and wired only once. If a prototype bean is injected directly as a constructor parameter, that one prototype instance is created at that single wiring moment and reused for the singleton's entire lifetime — defeating the purpose of prototype scope.

faq.q: What's the difference between request and session scope?
faq.a: Request scope creates one bean instance per HTTP request, discarded when the request ends. Session scope creates one instance per HTTP session, shared across every request from that same user until the session expires.

summary: Every bean passes through five stages: construct, inject, `@PostConstruct`, ready, `@PreDestroy`.
Scope decides how many instances exist: singleton (one), prototype (new every time), or web-aware scopes.
Injecting a prototype bean directly into a singleton silently freezes it to one instance — use `ObjectProvider` or a scoped proxy instead.
quickRevision: Bean lifecycle order: constructor → dependencies injected → `@PostConstruct` → ready → `@PreDestroy`.
`@PostConstruct` runs once, after all dependencies are set.
`@PreDestroy` runs once, on graceful shutdown only.
`singleton` = one instance per container (default).
`prototype` = new instance every time it's requested.
`request`/`session`/`application` = one instance per HTTP request/session/ServletContext.
Prototype-in-singleton gotcha: only one prototype instance ever gets created, at wiring time.
Fix: `ObjectProvider<T>` or a scoped proxy (`@Scope(proxyMode = ScopedProxyMode.TARGET_CLASS)`).

beginner: What are the main stages of a Spring bean's lifecycle?
What is the default bean scope?
What does `@PostConstruct` do?
intermediate: What is the difference between singleton and prototype scope?
Why doesn't `@PreDestroy` always run?
What's the difference between request and session scope?
advanced: Explain the prototype-in-singleton problem and two ways to fix it.
How does a scoped proxy solve the prototype-in-singleton problem internally?
At what exact point in the bean lifecycle can you safely rely on an injected dependency being non-null?

relatedTopics.label: DI & IoC — how dependencies are injected before PostConstruct runs
Core Spring & Spring Boot — how the container is built at startup
Spring Data JPA — repository beans created through this same pipeline

title: Configuration & Profiles
overview: Spring Boot applications need configuration that changes per environment.
Properties, profiles, and Actuator are the tools that make this possible.
This page covers setting config values, switching behavior per environment, and monitoring a running app.
why: The same code runs differently in development, staging, and production.
A database URL, a feature flag, or a log level should never be hardcoded.
Spring needs a way to externalize these values and swap them per environment, without touching code.
problem: Hardcoding a database URL directly in Java code means a code change and a redeploy for every environment.
A single properties file can't hold different values for dev, staging, and prod at once.
Without a clear override order, it's unclear which config value actually wins when several sources set the same key.

intro: Configuration, precedence, and profiles solve each part of that problem in turn.

topics.heading: Property Configuration
points: **`application.yml`** — the default config file; supports nested, hierarchical structure.
**`@Value`** — injects a single property. `@Value("${app.name:default}")` — the `:default` part is a fallback.
**`@ConfigurationProperties(prefix = "app")`** — type-safe binding of a whole related group of properties onto one class.
**`@PropertySource`** — loads an extra property file beyond the default `application.yml`.

topics.heading: Profiles
points: **Activating** — `spring.profiles.active=dev`, or `SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE=prod` as an environment variable.
**Profile-specific files** — `application-dev.yml` is automatically layered on top of the base `application.yml` when `dev` is active.
**`@Profile("dev")`** on a `@Bean` method — that bean is only created when the `dev` profile is active.
**Multiple profiles at once** — `spring.profiles.active=dev,metrics` activates both simultaneously.

topics.heading: Actuator & Monitoring
points: **`spring-boot-starter-actuator`** — adds operational HTTP endpoints, with zero custom code.
**Key endpoints** — `/actuator/health`, `/actuator/info`, `/actuator/metrics`, `/actuator/env`, `/actuator/beans`.
**Custom health check** — implement `HealthIndicator` to report whether a downstream dependency is reachable.
**Securing it** — expose only what's needed with `management.endpoints.web.exposure.include`.

topics.heading: Behind the Scenes: How Property Resolution Actually Works
points: **`Environment`** — the abstraction that actually holds and resolves every property; `@Value` and `@ConfigurationProperties` both delegate to it under the hood.
**`PropertySource`** — each individual source — command-line args, env vars, one specific yml file — is wrapped as one `PropertySource`.
**`PropertySources`** — an ordered list of every `PropertySource`, held inside `Environment`, in exactly the precedence order shown above.
**`PropertySourcesPlaceholderConfigurer`** — resolves `${...}` placeholders by walking that ordered list top to bottom, returning the first source that has the key.
The precedence diagram above isn't a separate rule Spring enforces — it's just the order `PropertySources` happens to hold those sources in.

topics.heading: Production Notes
points: Inject secrets as **environment variables**, or pull them from a secrets manager at startup — never commit them to any `application*.yml` file.
Keep **`management.endpoints.web.exposure.include`** explicit and reviewed on every change, instead of defaulting to exposing every Actuator endpoint.
Add **`@Validated`** to a `@ConfigurationProperties` class so a missing required value fails the app at **startup**, not later at runtime.
For multi-service setups, a centralized option like **Spring Cloud Config Server** or **Vault** can serve `application.yml` externally — worth knowing exists, rarely worth detailing in an interview.

architectureFlow.mermaid:
graph TD
    A["Command-line arguments (highest)"] --> B["Environment variables"]
    B --> C["application-profile.yml"]
    C --> D["application.yml"]
    D --> E["PropertySource / code defaults (lowest)"]
architectureFlow.caption: Same property, set in two places? The one higher in this chain wins.

simpleExample.scenario: Reading a feature flag with a safe fallback if it's set nowhere at all.
simpleExample.input: No `feature.new-checkout` key exists in any config file or environment variable.
simpleExample.execution: Spring searches the whole property source chain for `feature.new-checkout`, finds nothing, and falls back to the default after the colon.
simpleExample.explanation: The `:false` is a fallback value, used only when the property is missing from every source.

realWorldExample.scenario: Binding a whole payment-gateway config block, with different timeouts per environment.
realWorldExample.execution: Spring layers `application-prod.yml` on top of `application.yml`. Both define `gateway.*`, so the prod file's values win for every key it defines.
realWorldExample.output: `GatewayProperties.baseUrl()` is `https://api.gateway.com` and `timeoutMs()` is `2000` — the production values, automatically.
realWorldExample.explanation: One typed record replaces a whole set of separate `@Value` fields, and the profile-specific file overrides only the keys it redefines.

comparisonTables.title: @Value vs @ConfigurationProperties
rows: A whole group of related properties
One typed class for the whole group
A cohesive config block, e.g. one integration's settings

bestPractices: Use `@ConfigurationProperties` for any config block with more than 2-3 related values.
Keep secrets out of `application.yml` — inject them as environment variables instead.
Expose only the Actuator endpoints actually needed in production.
commonMistakes: Assuming `application-dev.yml` alone is enough, without ever setting `spring.profiles.active` to `dev`.
Exposing `/actuator/env` or `/actuator/beans` publicly — they can leak secrets and internal structure.
Not realizing an environment variable silently overrides a value already set in `application.yml`.
interviewTips: Be ready to recite the property precedence order from memory: command-line args, env vars, profile-specific yml, base yml, defaults.
If asked about securing Actuator, mention `management.endpoints.web.exposure.include` by name.

faq.q: Can more than one profile be active at the same time?
faq.a: Yes. `spring.profiles.active=dev,metrics` activates both the `dev` and `metrics` profiles simultaneously; their profile-specific files are layered together.

faq.q: Does application-prod.yml replace application.yml entirely?
faq.a: No. It's layered on top — only the keys it defines override the base file. Everything else still comes from `application.yml`.

faq.q: Why shouldn't I expose all Actuator endpoints in production?
faq.a: Endpoints like `/actuator/env` and `/actuator/beans` can reveal secrets, internal configuration, and application structure to anyone who can reach the server.

summary: Property configuration lets the same code behave differently per environment, without code changes.
When the same property is set in multiple places, a strict precedence order decides which value wins.
Profiles activate environment-specific config files and beans; Actuator exposes operational visibility into a running app.
quickRevision: `application.yml` is the default config file; YAML supports nested structure.
`@Value` injects one property, with an optional `:default` fallback.
`@ConfigurationProperties` binds a whole group of related properties to one class.
Precedence, highest to lowest: command-line args, env vars, `application-{profile}.yml`, `application.yml`, defaults.
`spring.profiles.active` (or `SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE`) activates a profile.
`@Profile("dev")` on a `@Bean` method creates that bean only for that profile.
`spring-boot-starter-actuator` exposes `/actuator/health`, `/info`, `/metrics`, `/env`, `/beans`.
Only expose the Actuator endpoints actually needed — never all of them, publicly.

beginner: What's the difference between `application.properties` and `application.yml`?
What does `@Value("${app.name:default}")` do?
What is a Spring profile?
intermediate: What is the property source precedence order?
How would you bind a whole group of related properties to one class?
How do you activate a Spring profile in a container environment?
advanced: How would you structure config for a service that needs different database credentials in dev, staging, and prod?
What are the security risks of exposing Actuator endpoints publicly, and how do you mitigate them?
How would you write a custom `HealthIndicator` that checks a downstream payment gateway's availability?

relatedTopics.label: Core Spring & Spring Boot — how configuration classes are processed at startup
Spring Data JPA — datasource configuration in practice
JioOnePay Architecture Layers — externalized config used for live routing changes

title: Spring Data JPA
overview: Spring Data JPA is a library that removes the need to hand-write database access code.
You define an interface. Spring generates the working implementation for you, at runtime.
It sits on top of JPA and Hibernate — it does not replace them.
why: Every Java backend needs to read and write data.
Doing that by hand, for every table, is repetitive.
Spring Data JPA removes that repetition.
problem: **Raw JDBC** — you write the SQL string yourself.
You manually map each `ResultSet` column to a Java field.
You manually open and close the `Connection`, `Statement`, and `ResultSet`.
Forgetting to close one of these leaks a database connection.
**Hibernate/JPA alone** — the object-to-row mapping is now automatic.
But you still hand-write a `UserDao` class per entity.
That DAO class looks almost identical for every entity: `save()`, `findById()`, `findAll()`.
**Spring Data JPA's fix** — you write one interface: `interface UserRepository extends JpaRepository<User, Long> {}`.
Spring generates the implementation for you, at runtime.
`save()`, `findById()`, `findAll()`, `deleteById()` all exist with zero code from you.

intro: Spring Data JPA is built in layers. Each layer has one job.
The diagram below shows the full chain, top to bottom.

topics.heading: Entity & Mapping Annotations
points: **`@Entity`** marks a class as a database-backed object.
`@Entity @Table(name = "orders") class Order { ... }`.
The class needs a no-argument constructor.
**`@Id`** marks the primary key field.
**`@GeneratedValue`** tells the database how to generate that key. Common strategies: `IDENTITY` (auto-increment), `SEQUENCE`, `UUID`.
**`@Column`** configures one column: `@Column(name = "order_status", nullable = false, length = 20)`.
**`@Enumerated(EnumType.STRING)`** stores an enum as its name, not its position.
Always use `STRING`, never `ORDINAL`.
`ORDINAL` stores the enum's index number (0, 1, 2...).
Reordering the enum's constants later silently corrupts old `ORDINAL` data.
**Auditing** — `@CreatedDate`, `@LastModifiedDate`, `@CreatedBy` add automatic timestamps.
Turn this on with `@EnableJpaAuditing` on a configuration class.

topics.heading: Relationship Annotations
points: **`@OneToMany`** — one parent has many children. Example: one `Merchant` has many `Order`s.
**`@ManyToOne`** — the reverse side. Many `Order`s belong to one `Merchant`.
`@OneToMany(mappedBy = "merchant") List<Order> orders;` on `Merchant`.
`@ManyToOne @JoinColumn(name = "merchant_id") Merchant merchant;` on `Order`.
**`@ManyToMany`** — both sides have many of each other. Example: a `Product` has many `Tag`s, a `Tag` labels many `Product`s.
`@ManyToMany @JoinTable(name = "product_tags")`.
Prefer `Set` over `List` here — `List` can trigger Hibernate's "multiple bag" fetch error.
**`@OneToOne`** — exactly one match on each side. Example: one `User` has one `Profile`.
**`FetchType.LAZY`** loads the related data only when you actually access it.
**`FetchType.EAGER`** loads it immediately, every time, whether you need it or not.
Default for collections is `LAZY`. Prefer `LAZY` almost always — `EAGER` silently adds extra data to every query.
**`CascadeType`** propagates an action from the parent to its children automatically.
`ALL`, `PERSIST`, `MERGE`, `REMOVE` are the common options.

topics.heading: Types of Queries
points: **Derived (method-name) queries** — Spring reads the method name and builds the query from it.
`findByStatusAndMerchantId(...)`, `findByEmailContaining(...)`.
No annotation needed. No SQL written.
**JPQL** — write a query yourself using entity names, with `@Query`.
`@Query("SELECT o FROM Order o WHERE o.status = :status")`.
Works against your Java entity and field names, not raw table/column names.
**Native SQL** — write real SQL, with `@Query(nativeQuery = true)`.
Use this only when JPQL can't express what you need, e.g. a database-specific function.
**`Specification` (Criteria API)** — build a query at runtime by combining conditions.
Use this when the filters change based on user input, instead of writing one derived method per combination.
**Pagination** — `Page<Order> findByStatus(Status s, Pageable p);`.
Called as `repo.findByStatus(PAID, PageRequest.of(0, 20));`.
`Page` also returns the total number of results. Use `Slice` instead if you don't need that count — it's a cheaper query.

topics.heading: Transactions & Performance
points: **`@Transactional`** marks a method as one database transaction.
Default behavior: joins an existing transaction, or starts a new one.
It rolls back automatically on an unchecked exception (`RuntimeException`).
It does **not** roll back on a checked exception, by default.
**`@Transactional(readOnly = true)`** — use this on methods that only read data.
It skips Hibernate's change-tracking, which is a real performance win.
**The N+1 problem** — the most common Spring Data JPA performance bug.
Fetching `N` parent rows with a `LAZY` child collection runs 1 query for the parents.
Then it runs one more query **per parent**, to fetch that parent's children.
Total: `1 + N` queries, where a single join query would have done the job.
**Fix** — `@EntityGraph`, `JOIN FETCH` in JPQL, or `@BatchSize`.

topics.heading: Behind the Scenes: How JpaRepository Gets Its Implementation
points: `OrderRepository extends JpaRepository<Order, Long>` has no implementation class anywhere in the codebase — Spring builds one at runtime, not compile time.
**`RepositoryFactoryBeanSupport`** is a `FactoryBean` — instead of Spring instantiating `OrderRepository` directly, this factory bean's job is to construct the actual repository bean.
That factory builds a JDK **dynamic proxy** for the `OrderRepository` interface — this proxy is the object your `@Service` actually gets injected with.
Every call on that proxy is delegated to **`SimpleJpaRepository`**, the real class that implements `save()`, `findById()`, `deleteById()` using an injected `EntityManager` underneath.
A derived query method like `findByStatusAndMerchantId(...)` is parsed into an actual query **once, at startup** by this same proxy — not re-parsed on every call.

topics.heading: Production Notes
points: Tune `spring.jpa.properties.hibernate.jdbc.batch_size` so bulk inserts/updates go out as batched statements instead of one round trip per row.

**`spring.jpa.open-in-view`** defaults to `true`, keeping the Hibernate session open through view rendering — many teams disable it explicitly to avoid lazy-loading exceptions surfacing as confusing errors at the wrong layer.
Never rely on `spring.jpa.hibernate.ddl-auto=update` in production — use a migration tool like **Flyway** or **Liquibase** so schema changes are versioned and reviewable.
Spring Boot auto-configures **HikariCP** as the connection pool — sizing it (`maximum-pool-size`) correctly matters more than most people expect under real load.

architectureFlow.mermaid:
graph TD A[Your Code] --> B[Spring Data JPA
JpaRepository - no boilerplate] B --> C[JPA
a specification: Entity, EntityManager, JPQL] C --> D[Hibernate
the JPA provider - the real ORM engine] D --> E[JDBC
low-level Java database API] E --> F[(Database)]
architectureFlow.caption: ORM is the general pattern. JPA is Java's standard interface for it. Hibernate is the most common implementation of that standard.

simpleExample.scenario: Saving a new order with zero repository implementation code.
simpleExample.input: A new `Order` object with `status = PENDING`, not yet saved.
simpleExample.execution: `orderRepository.save(order)` runs. Spring Data JPA's generated proxy calls Hibernate, which builds and runs an `INSERT` statement through JDBC.
simpleExample.output: The same `Order` object, now with its generated `id` field populated from the database.
simpleExample.explanation: `OrderRepository` has no implementation class anywhere in the codebase — Spring generates one automatically because it extends `JpaRepository`.

realWorldExample.scenario: Finding all paid orders for one merchant, paginated, without writing any SQL.
realWorldExample.input: `merchantId = 42`, `status = PAID`, first page, 20 results per page.
realWorldExample.execution: Spring parses the method name into a query filtering by `merchantId` and `status`, adds `LIMIT`/`OFFSET` for the requested page, and runs it.
realWorldExample.output: A `Page<Order>` containing up to 20 matching `Order` rows, plus the total count of matching rows across all pages.
realWorldExample.explanation: This method needed zero SQL and zero `@Query` annotation — the method name alone was enough for Spring to build the query.

comparisonTables.title: JPA vs Hibernate
rows: No — it defines a contract only
None if you code to the JPA interfaces

comparisonTables.title: JPQL vs Native Query
rows: Tied to one database's SQL dialect
Only when JPQL can't express the query
`SELECT o FROM Order o WHERE o.status = :status`

bestPractices: Default every relationship to `FetchType.LAZY`, and only widen to `EAGER` with a specific, measured reason.
Use `@Transactional(readOnly = true)` on every method that only reads data.
Use DTO or interface projections when you only need a few columns, not the whole entity.
commonMistakes: Using `EnumType.ORDINAL` — it silently breaks if the enum's constant order ever changes.
Looping over a parent list and touching a `LAZY` child collection on each one — this is the N+1 problem.
Catching a checked exception inside a `@Transactional` method and swallowing it — the transaction won't roll back.
interviewTips: If asked "what's the difference between JPA and Hibernate", lead with: JPA is a specification, Hibernate is an implementation of it.
If asked about performance problems in JPA, N+1 is almost always the expected answer.
Be ready to name at least two fixes for N+1: `JOIN FETCH` and `@EntityGraph`.

faq.q: Can I use Spring Data JPA without Hibernate?
faq.a: Yes. Spring Data JPA depends on the JPA specification, not Hibernate directly. Swapping in a different JPA provider like EclipseLink is possible, though Hibernate is the default and by far the most common choice.

faq.q: Why does `Page` return a total count but `Slice` doesn't?
faq.a: `Page` runs an extra `COUNT` query to know the total number of matching rows across every page. `Slice` skips that extra query — cheaper, but it only knows whether there's a next page, not the total.

faq.q: Does `@Transactional` roll back on every exception?
faq.a: No. By default it rolls back on unchecked exceptions (`RuntimeException` and its subclasses) only. A checked exception needs `@Transactional(rollbackFor = Exception.class)` to also trigger a rollback.

summary: Spring Data JPA removes repository boilerplate by generating implementations at runtime.
It's built on JPA (the specification), which Hibernate implements (the ORM engine), which runs on JDBC.
Its query system covers derived methods, JPQL, native SQL, and dynamic `Specification` queries.
The most common real-world bug is the N+1 problem, caused by `LAZY` collections accessed in a loop.
quickRevision: Spring Data JPA generates repository implementations at runtime — you never write the implementation class.
JPA is a specification. Hibernate is an implementation of that specification.
The full chain: Spring Data JPA → JPA → Hibernate → JDBC → Database.
`@Enumerated(EnumType.STRING)` — never use `ORDINAL`.
`FetchType.LAZY` is the safer default; `EAGER` silently over-fetches.
Derived queries build SQL from a method name — no annotation needed.
`@Query` supports both JPQL (entity-based) and native SQL (`nativeQuery = true`).
N+1 = 1 query for parents + 1 extra query per parent's lazy collection.
Fix N+1 with `JOIN FETCH`, `@EntityGraph`, or `@BatchSize`.
`@Transactional` rolls back on unchecked exceptions by default, not checked ones.

beginner: What is Spring Data JPA, in one sentence?
What does `JpaRepository` give you for free?
What's the difference between `@OneToMany` and `@ManyToOne`?
intermediate: What is the difference between JPA and Hibernate?
What is the N+1 problem, and how would you detect it?
When would you write a native query instead of JPQL?
advanced: How does `@Transactional` propagation work when one `@Transactional` method calls another in the same class?
Why can `List`-typed `@ManyToMany` relationships cause a Hibernate "multiple bag" exception, and how does switching to `Set` fix it?
How would you design pagination for an endpoint that must support both fast repeated calls and an accurate total count?

relatedTopics.label: Bean Lifecycle & Scopes — how the repository bean itself is created
Indexing — the database side of query performance
JioOnePay Service Details — a real transaction/amount table schema

title: Spring Security
overview: Spring Security controls who can access an application, and what they're allowed to do once they're in.
It works in two separate steps: authentication, then authorization.
This page covers session-based login, stateless JWT authentication, and access control.
why: A backend can't let every request through unchecked.
Some endpoints need to verify who is calling.
Some endpoints need to restrict access further, even for a known, logged-in user.
problem: Without a security framework, every controller would need its own manual login check.
Password handling — hashing, comparing, storing — would be reimplemented by hand in every project.
Access rules like "only admins can call this" would end up scattered across the codebase as ad-hoc `if` checks.

intro: Authentication answers "who are you". Authorization answers "what can you do".

topics.heading: Authentication Basics
points: **`SecurityFilterChain`** — configure security via a `@Bean SecurityFilterChain filterChain(HttpSecurity http)` method.
This replaced the older `WebSecurityConfigurerAdapter` class-extension style.
**`UserDetailsService`** — implement `loadUserByUsername(String username)` to fetch a user from your own database.
**`PasswordEncoder`** — always `BCryptPasswordEncoder`, never plain text.
**Form login** — classic session-based, browser-rendered login.
**HTTP Basic** — credentials sent Base64-encoded on every request; only ever use this over HTTPS.

topics.heading: JWT Structure & Mechanics
points: **JWT structure** — `Header.Payload.Signature`, each Base64-encoded and dot-separated.
The Header names the signing algorithm. The Payload holds claims (user id, roles, expiry). The Signature proves it wasn't tampered with.
**Why JWT** — it's stateless. The server doesn't store a session for each logged-in user, so it scales horizontally without shared session storage.
**Token generation** — set a subject, custom claims, and an expiration, then sign with a secret key.
**JWT filter** — extend `OncePerRequestFilter`, extract the token from the `Authorization` header, validate it, and populate `SecurityContext`.
**Refresh tokens** — pair a short-lived access token with a long-lived refresh token, rotated on each use.

topics.heading: Authorization
points: **URL-based** — `.requestMatchers("/admin/**").hasRole("ADMIN")` — coarse-grained, decided before the request reaches a controller.
**Method security** — `@PreAuthorize("#id == principal.id")` directly on a method — fine-grained, can reference the method's own arguments.
**Role vs Authority** — a Role (`ROLE_ADMIN`) is a coarse label; an Authority (`READ_PRIVILEGE`) is fine-grained. A Role is really just an Authority with a naming convention.
**CORS** — controls which browser origins, methods, and headers are allowed to call your API.

topics.heading: Behind the Scenes: How a Request Actually Gets Authenticated
points: The servlet container only ever registers one filter for Spring Security — **`DelegatingFilterProxy`**, bound to the name `springSecurityFilterChain`.
That proxy forwards every request into Spring's own **`FilterChainProxy`** — the real container holding the ordered list of security filters.

That list typically includes `UsernamePasswordAuthenticationFilter`, a JWT filter if one is configured, `ExceptionTranslationFilter`, and `FilterSecurityInterceptor` (or `AuthorizationFilter` in newer versions).
A successful filter calls **`AuthenticationManager.authenticate(...)`**, which delegates to one or more `AuthenticationProvider`s to actually verify the credentials.
On success, the resulting `Authentication` object is stored in **`SecurityContextHolder`** — thread-local by default — for the rest of that request.
Controllers and `@PreAuthorize` checks read the principal from `SecurityContextHolder`, not by re-parsing the token or session again.

topics.heading: Production Notes
points: `SecurityContextHolder`'s default **thread-local** strategy doesn't survive onto a different thread — an `@Async` method or a separate thread pool needs `MODE_INHERITABLETHREADLOCAL`, or the context propagated manually.
Stateless JWT setups explicitly set `SessionCreationPolicy.STATELESS`, since there's no server-side session to create in the first place.

CORS and CSRF misconfiguration is one of the most common sources of confusing `403`s in real deployments — CSRF is normally disabled for stateless JWT APIs but must stay enabled for cookie/session-based browser clients.

**Mutual TLS (mTLS)** is a complementary authentication mechanism for service-to-service calls, used instead of (or alongside) tokens — see JioOnePay's Gateway Integration Layer, where bank-grade external calls (e.g. Citi) authenticate via a PKCS12 client certificate over TLS 1.2.

architectureFlow.mermaid:
graph TD
    A["Client sends login credentials"] --> B["Server validates and issues JWT"]
    B --> C["Client stores the token"]
    C --> D["Client sends request with Bearer token"]
    D --> E["JWT filter validates the token"]
    E --> F["Controller runs, request treated as authenticated"]
architectureFlow.caption: Login happens once. Every request after that carries its own proof of identity.

simpleExample.scenario: Restricting one endpoint to authenticated users only.
simpleExample.input: A request to `GET /orders/42` with no `Authorization` header.
simpleExample.execution: The filter chain checks the rule for `/orders/**`, sees `authenticated()` is required, and finds no valid credentials on the request.
simpleExample.output: A `401 Unauthorized` response, before the request ever reaches `OrderController`.
simpleExample.explanation: The security check happens in a filter, ahead of the controller — the controller method never runs for an unauthenticated request.

realWorldExample.scenario: Letting a merchant cancel only their own orders, not anyone else's.
realWorldExample.input: A logged-in merchant with `merchantId = 7` calls `cancelOrder(merchantId = 9, orderId = 100)`.
realWorldExample.execution: `@PreAuthorize` evaluates before the method body runs, comparing the `merchantId` argument (9) against the logged-in principal's own `merchantId` (7).
realWorldExample.output: An `AccessDeniedException` is thrown; `cancelOrder`'s body never executes.
realWorldExample.explanation: URL-based rules alone can't express "only your own resource" — that level of detail needs method security, which can reference the actual arguments and the logged-in principal.

comparisonTables.title: Session-Based Auth vs JWT
rows: Server stores a session per user
Needs shared session storage across servers
Scales horizontally with no shared state
Harder — the token is valid until it expires

comparisonTables.title: URL-Based vs Method Security
rows: Before the request reaches a controller
Right before the method body runs

bestPractices: Always hash passwords with `BCryptPasswordEncoder` — never store or compare plain text.
Prefer JWT for APIs consumed by mobile apps or other services; prefer sessions for classic server-rendered apps.
Use method security for anything that depends on "is this the caller's own resource".
commonMistakes: Sending JWTs or Basic Auth credentials over plain HTTP instead of HTTPS.
Relying only on URL-based rules when the real requirement is per-resource ownership.
Storing a JWT secret key in source code instead of a secrets manager or environment variable.
interviewTips: Separate authentication ("who are you") from authorization ("what can you do") clearly when explaining Spring Security.
Be ready to explain why JWT is called stateless, and what tradeoff that brings for revoking access early.

faq.q: Can I use both session-based login and JWT in the same application?
faq.a: Yes — some apps use session-based login for server-rendered admin pages and JWT for a public API, configured as separate SecurityFilterChain beans matched to different URL patterns.

faq.q: How do you revoke a JWT before it expires?
faq.a: JWTs can't be individually invalidated by themselves, since the server doesn't track them. Common workarounds: a short expiry time paired with a refresh token, or a server-side blocklist of revoked token IDs.

faq.q: What's the difference between a Role and an Authority in Spring Security?
faq.a: An Authority is a fine-grained permission string, like READ_PRIVILEGE. A Role is really just an Authority with a ROLE_ prefix by convention, treated as a coarser label like ROLE_ADMIN.

summary: Spring Security separates authentication (who you are) from authorization (what you can do).
Session-based login suits server-rendered apps; JWT suits stateless APIs and services.
URL-based rules handle coarse access control; method security with `@PreAuthorize` handles fine-grained, per-resource rules.
quickRevision: Authentication verifies identity. Authorization decides what an authenticated identity can do.
`SecurityFilterChain` configures security via a `@Bean` method, replacing `WebSecurityConfigurerAdapter`.
Always hash passwords with `BCryptPasswordEncoder`.
JWT = Header.Payload.Signature, stateless, scales horizontally with no shared session storage.
A JWT filter extends `OncePerRequestFilter` and populates `SecurityContext` on a valid token.
URL-based rules are coarse and run before the controller; method security with `@PreAuthorize` is fine-grained.
A Role is an Authority with a `ROLE_` prefix by convention.
CORS configuration controls which browser origins/methods/headers can call your API.

beginner: What's the difference between authentication and authorization?
What does `BCryptPasswordEncoder` do?
What is JWT, in one sentence?
intermediate: Why is JWT called stateless, and what's the tradeoff?
What's the difference between URL-based security and method security?
What's the difference between a Role and an Authority?
advanced: How would you revoke a compromised JWT before it naturally expires?
How would you design refresh token rotation to limit the damage from a leaked token?
How would you configure two separate `SecurityFilterChain`s — one for a server-rendered admin panel, one for a public JWT-secured API — in the same application?
